In the Prophetic report narrated by both groups: "There is no contagion (عدوى), no augury (طيرة), no owl-spirit (هامة), no misfortune (شؤم), no Safar-omen (صفر), no suckling after weaning, no returning to nomadism after migration, no silence for a whole day until night, no divorce before marriage, no manumission before ownership, and no orphanhood after maturity."

His saying, the Exalted: كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِالنُّذُرِ — "Thamud denied the warnings" (54:23). Warnings (النذر) is either a verbal noun, as has been said, meaning: Thamud denied the warning of their prophet Salih (عليه السلام); or it is the plural of 'nadhir' (نذير) in the sense of "the warner," meaning: Thamud denied the prophets, because their denial of one of them is a denial of all of them, as their message is one without difference. Thus, it would carry the same meaning as His saying: كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ الْمُرْسَلِينَ — "Thamud denied the apostles" (26:141). Or it is the plural of 'nadhir' in the sense of "warning," which returns to one of the two previous meanings.

His saying, the Exalted: فَقَالُوا أَبَشَرًا مِنَّا وَاحِدًا نَتَّبِعُهُ إِنَّا إِذًا لَفِي ضَلَالٍ وَسُعُرٍ — "They said, ‘Shall we follow a lone human from among ourselves? Then we would indeed be in error and madness’" (54:24). This is branched from the denial. Madness (السعر) is the plural of 'sa‘ir' (سعير) meaning a blazing fire. It has also been suggested to mean insanity, which is more suitable to the context. It appears that "lone" (واحدا) refers to numerical oneness. The meaning is: they denied him and said: "Shall we follow a human of our own kind who is a single person, having no retinue or groups with him? If we did, we would indeed be settled in a strange error and insanity."

Thus, this statement would be their justification for not following Salih due to his lack of numbers and power, as they were accustomed to following those who possessed such things, like kings and grandees. Salih (عليه السلام) was calling them to obey him and to reject the obedience of their grandees, as Allah the Glorified recounts from him: فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ وَلَا تُطِيعُوا أَمْرَ الْمُسْرِفِينَ — "So be wary of Allah and obey me, and do not obey the dictates of the profligate" (26:151-152).

If "lone" were taken as "one of a kind," the meaning would be: "Shall we follow a human who is one of us—that is, like us and of our kind?" The following verse would then be an explanation of it.

His saying, the Exalted: أَأُلْقِيَ الذِّكْرُ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ بَيْنِنَا بَلْ هُوَ كَذَّابٌ أَشِرٌ — "Has the Reminder been cast upon him from among us? Rather he is an insolent liar" (54:25). The interrogation, like the previous one, is for denial. The meaning is: "Has the revelation been sent down upon him and he been singled out with it from among us, when he has no merit over us? That can never be." The use of "casting" (الإلقاء) rather than "sending down" or the like is to suggest haste, as has been said.

It is possible that the meaning is to negate that he should be singled out with the casting of the Reminder from among them when he is a human like them. If revelation were true and it were possible for it to descend upon a human, it would have descended upon all humans; so why was he singled out with that which is of a nature to be granted to everyone? Thus, the verse would carry the same meaning as their saying to him: مَا أَنْتَ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُنَا — "You are nothing but a human like us" (26:154).

His saying: بَلْ هُوَ كَذَّابٌ أَشِرٌ — "Rather he is an insolent liar" means one of extreme arrogance and pride, who wishes to make himself great over us by this means.

His saying, the Exalted: سَيَعْلَمُونَ غَدًا مَنِ الْكَذَّابُ الْأَشِرُ — "Tomorrow they will know who is the insolent liar" (54:26). This is a recounting of the speech of the Glorified to Salih (عليه السلام), as are the two verses following it.

What is meant by "tomorrow" (غدا) is the ultimate outcome, from the saying: "Indeed, with today is a tomorrow." The Glorified alludes by it to the punishment that will descend upon them, at which point they will know with the knowledge of direct vision (علم عيان) who the insolent liar is—Salih or themselves?

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا مُرْسِلُو النَّاقَةِ فِتْنَةً لَهُمْ فَارْتَقِبْهُمْ وَاصْطَبِرْ — "We are sending the she-camel as a trial for them; so watch them and be patient" (54:27). This is in the position of providing the reason for what He informed—that the punishment will descend upon them. The sense is: the punishment will descend upon them because We are doing such-and-such. Trial (الفتنة) means examination and testing. The meaning is: We are sending—by way of miracle—the she-camel they requested as a test for them; so wait for them and be patient with their harm.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنَبِّئْهُمْ أَنَّ الْمَاءَ قِسْمَةٌ بَيْنَهُمْ كُلُّ شِرْبٍ مُحْتَضَرٌ — "And inform them that the water is to be shared between them; each share of water being attended [by the turn-holder]" (54:28). The first plural pronoun refers to the people, and the second to both the people and the she-camel by way of predominance (التغليب). Sharing (القسمة) means that which is divided. Share of water (الشرب) is the portion of drinking water. The meaning is: and inform them after the sending of the she-camel that the water is divided between the people and the she-camel; each portion of the drinking water shall be attended by its owner. Thus, the people attend during their turn and the she-camel during its turn. The Exalted says: قَالَ هَٰذِهِ نَاقَةٌ لَهَا شِرْبٌ وَلَكُمْ شِرْبُ يَوْمٍ مَعْلُومٍ — "He said, ‘This is a she-camel: she shall have a [day’s] share of water, and you shall have a [day’s] share of water on a specified day’" (26:155).

His saying, the Exalted: فَنَادَوْا صَاحِبَهُمْ فَتَعَاطَىٰ فَعَقَرَ — "Then they called their companion, and he took [the sword] and hamstrung [her]" (54:29). What is meant by "their companion" is the one who hamstrung the she-camel. Taking (التعاطي) means reaching for or grasping. The meaning is: the people called the one who would hamstring the she-camel to do so, and he reached for the act of hamstringing and hamstrung and killed her.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ صَيْحَةً وَاحِدَةً فَكَانُوا كَهَشِيمِ الْمُحْتَظِرِ — "So how were My punishment and My warnings? Indeed We sent against them a single Cry, and they became like the dry sticks of a corral-builder" (54:30-31). The corral-builder (المحتظر) is the owner of a corral (حظيرة), which is like a wall made to keep livestock. The dry sticks (هشيم) of the corral-builder are the dry wood and the like that the owner of the corral gathers for his livestock. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا — etc. (54:32). Its commentary has already been provided.

His saying, the Exalted: كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ لُوطٍ بِالنُّذُرِ — "The people of Lot denied the warnings" (54:33). Its commentary has been provided in its counterpart.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ حَاصِبًا إِلَّا آلَ لُوطٍ نَجَّيْنَاهُمْ بِسَحَرٍ — "Indeed We sent a rain of stones upon them, except the family of Lot, whom We delivered at dawn" (54:34). A rain of stones (الحاصب) is a wind that brings stones and pebbles; what is meant is the wind that was sent and pelted them with stones of baked clay (سجيل منضود).

It is stated in al-Majma‘ al-Bayan: "The word 'sahar' (سحر), when it is indefinite, refers to one of the dawns. It is said: 'I saw Zayd at a dawn (سحرا) among dawns.' But if you mean the dawn of your current day, you say: 'I came to him at the dawn (بسحر)'—with a 'fatha'—and 'I came to him at dawn (سحر)'—without nunation." The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: نِعْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِنَا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِي مَنْ شَكَرَ — "as a blessing from Us. Thus do We reward those who give thanks" (54:35). "Blessing" (نعمة) is an object of purpose (مفعول له) for "We delivered them"; that is, We delivered them so that it might be a blessing from Us with which We specifically favored them because they were thankful to Us, and the reward for thankfulness to Us is salvation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَنْذَرَهُمْ بَطْشَتَنَا فَتَمَارَوْا بِالنُّذُرِ — "He had certainly warned them of Our seizing, but they disputed the warnings" (54:36). The subject pronoun in "he warned them" refers to Lut (عليه السلام). Seizing (البطشة) is a severe taking with punishment. Disputing (التماري) is persistence in argumentation and casting doubt. Warnings (النذر) means the act of warning. The meaning is: I swear, Lut frightened them with Our severe taking, but they argued against his warning and intimidation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ رَاوَدُوهُ عَنْ ضَيْفِهِ فَطَمَسْنَا أَعْيُنَهُمْ فَذُوقُوا عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ — "They certainly solicited his guests from him. So We blotted out their eyes: ‘Now taste My punishment and My warnings!’" (54:37). Their soliciting him for his guests was their demand that he hand over his guests—who were the angels—to them. Blotting out (طمس) their eyes is their erasure. His saying: فَذُوقُوا عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ — "Now taste My punishment and My warnings!" is a shift (التفات) to addressing them for the purpose of intensification and rebuke. Warnings (النذر) is a verbal noun referring to that which the warning relates to, which is the punishment. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ صَبَّحَهُمْ بُكْرَةً عَذَابٌ مُسْتَقِرٌّ — "And certainly an unrelenting punishment visited them at dawn" (54:38). It is stated in al-Majma‘ al-Bayan: "His saying: 'at dawn' (بكرة) is an adverb of time. If it is definite, such that you mean the dawn of your day, you say: 'I came to him at dawn (بكرة) and in the morning (غدوة)' without declining them. But 'at dawn' here—having nunation—is indefinite." The "unrelenting" (مستقر) nature of the punishment refers to its befalling them and its not failing to reach them.

His saying, the Exalted: فَذُوقُوا عَذَابِي — to His saying — مِنْ مُدَّكِرٍ (54:39-40). Its commentary has already been provided.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ جَاءَ آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ النُّذُرُ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا كُلِّهَا فَأَخَذْنَاهُمْ أَخْذَ عَزِيزٍ مُقْتَدِرٍ — "Certainly the warnings came to the clan of Pharaoh. They denied all Our signs. So We seized them with the seizing of One mighty and omnipotent" (54:41-42). Warnings (النذر) means the act of warning. His saying: كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا — "They denied Our signs" is separated without coordination because it is an answer to a presumed question. It is as if when it was said: "Certainly the warnings came to the clan of Pharaoh," it was asked: "So what did they do?" and it was answered with: "They denied Our signs," and from this was branched His saying: "So We seized them with the seizing of One mighty and omnipotent."

( Hadith Discourse )

In Ruh al-Ma‘ani, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ: "Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas: 'Had Allah not made it easy upon the tongues of human beings, no one from creation would have been able to speak the speech of Allah the Exalted.'"

He said: "And al-Daylami narrated a 'marfu‘' report from Anas similar to it." Then he said: "Perhaps the report of Anas, if authentic, is not a commentary on the verse."

I say: It is not far-fetched that the intended meaning is the second one we provided in the commentary of the verse.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: فَفَتَحْنَا أَبْوَابَ السَّمَاءِ بِمَاءٍ مُنْهَمِرٍ, he said: "A pouring without drops." وَفَجَّرْنَا الْأَرْضَ عُيُونًا فَالْتَقَى الْمَاءُ, he said: "The water of the sky and the water of the earth." عَلَىٰ أَمْرٍ قَدْ قُدِرَ وَحَمَلْنَاهُ, meaning Nuh, عَلَىٰ ذَاتِ أَلْواحٍ وَدُسُرٍ, he said: "The planks are the ship and the 'dusur' are the nails."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَنَادَوْا صَاحِبَهُمْ, he said: "Qudar, who hamstrung the she-camel." And regarding His saying: كَهَشِيمِ, he said: "Dry grass and plants."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abu Yazid from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) in a hadith mentioning the story of the people of Lut, he said: "They overwhelmed him—meaning Lut—until they entered the house. Then Jibra’il cried out to him and said: 'O Lut, leave them.' When they entered, Jibra’il gestured with his finger toward them and their eyes vanished, and that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فَطَمَسْنَا أَعْيُنَهُمْ."

Ayahs

أَكُفَّارُكُمْ خَيْرٌ مِّنْ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكُمْ أَمْ لَكُم بَرَآءَةٌ فِى ٱلزُّبُرِ (٤٣)
Are your faithless better than those? Have you [been granted] [some sort of] immunity in the scriptures? (43)
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ نَحْنُ جَمِيعٌ مُّنتَصِرٌ (٤٤)
Do they say, ‘We are a confederate league’? (44)
سَيُهْزَمُ ٱلْجَمْعُ وَيُوَلُّونَ ٱلدُّبُرَ (٤٥)
The league will be routed and turn its back [to flee]. (45)
بَلِ ٱلسَّاعَةُ مَوْعِدُهُمْ وَٱلسَّاعَةُ أَدْهَىٰ وَأَمَرُّ (٤٦)
Indeed, the Hour is their tryst; and the Hour will be most calamitous and bitter. (46)
إِنَّ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ وَسُعُرٍ (٤٧)
Indeed the guilty are steeped in error and madness. (47)
يَوْمَ يُسْحَبُونَ فِى ٱلنَّارِ عَلَىٰ وُجُوهِهِمْ ذُوقُوا۟ مَسَّ سَقَرَ (٤٨)
The day when they are dragged on their faces into the Fire, [it will be said to them,] ‘Taste the touch of hell!’ (48)
إِنَّا كُلَّ شَىْءٍ خَلَقْنَـٰهُ بِقَدَرٍ (٤٩)
Indeed We have created everything in a measure, (49)
وَمَآ أَمْرُنَآ إِلَّا وَٰحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍۭ بِٱلْبَصَرِ (٥٠)
and Our command is but a single [word], like the twinkling of an eye. (50)
وَلَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنَآ أَشْيَاعَكُمْ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ (٥١)
Certainly We have destroyed your likes. So is there anyone who will be admonished? (51)
وَكُلُّ شَىْءٍ فَعَلُوهُ فِى ٱلزُّبُرِ (٥٢)
Everything they have done is in the books, (52)
وَكُلُّ صَغِيرٍ وَكَبِيرٍ مُّسْتَطَرٌ (٥٣)
and everything big and small is committed to writing. (53)
إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَنَهَرٍ (٥٤)
Indeed the Godwary will be amid gardens and streams, (54)
فِى مَقْعَدِ صِدْقٍ عِندَ مَلِيكٍ مُّقْتَدِرٍۭ (٥٥)
in the abode of truthfulness with an omnipotent King. (55)

(Commentary)

The verses are in the sense of drawing a conclusion from the previously mentioned reports (الأنباء) that contain a deterrent (مزدجر). These include the report of the Hour (نبأ الساعة) mentioned first, followed by the reports of the perished nations mentioned second. Thus, the discourse first turns back to the reports of the perished nations, addressing the people of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by stating that their disbelievers are no better than those tyrannical and overbearing nations whom Allah destroyed in the most humiliating and effortless manner. Nor do they possess a written immunity (براءة) from Allah’s punishment, nor will their collective strength avail them in warding off retribution. Then, it turns back to the previously mentioned report of the Hour, identifying it as their difficult appointment if they commit crimes and deny the truth—and the Hour is more calamitous and more bitter. Finally, it points to the abode of the God-conscious (المتقين) on that Day, and with that, the Surah concludes.

His saying, the Exalted: «أَكُفَّارُكُمْ خَيْرٌ مِنْ أُولئِكُمْ أَمْ لَكُمْ بَراءَةٌ فِي الزُّبُرِ» — Are your disbelievers better than those, or do you have an immunity in the scriptures? (54:43). It appears to be an address to the people of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), both believer and disbeliever, as suggested by the possessive construction in "your disbelievers" (كُفَّارُكُمْ). "Betterness" (الخيرية) refers to superiority in worldly adornments and the trappings of life, such as wealth and children, or in terms of general character traits within their society, such as generosity, courage, and compassion for the weak. The demonstration "those" (أُولئِكُمْ) refers to the nations whose reports were mentioned: the people of Nuh, ‘Ad, Thamud, the people of Lut, and the folk of Pharaoh. The interrogation is for the purpose of negation (الإنكار).

The meaning is: those who disbelieve among you are not better than those perished and punished nations such that the punishment would encompass them but exclude you.

It is also possible that the address "your disbelievers" (أَكُفَّارُكُمْ) is specifically for the disbelievers, with the consideration that they are the people of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and among them are disbelievers, and they are those very individuals.

His saying: «أَمْ لَكُمْ بَراءَةٌ فِي الزُّبُرِ» — or do you have an immunity in the scriptures? Its apparent meaning also suggests a general address. The "scriptures" (الزبر) is the plural of Zabur (زبور), meaning a book. It has been mentioned that the intended meaning of al-Zubur is the heavenly books revealed to the prophets. The meaning is: Or do you have an immunity in the heavenly books revealed from Allah, stating that you are safe from punishment and accountability even if you disbelieve, commit crimes, and perpetrate whatever sins you wish?

His saying, the Exalted: «أَمْ يَقُولُونَ نَحْنُ جَمِيعٌ مُنْتَصِرٌ» — Or do they say, ‘We are a confederacy, and we shall prevail’? (54:44). The "confederacy" (الجميع) refers to the collective whole, meaning the unity of their society in terms of will and action. "Prevailing" (الانتصار) means taking revenge or mutual assistance, as in the addresses of the Day of Resurrection: «ما لَكُمْ لا تَناصَرُونَ» — ‘What is the matter with you that you do not help one another?’ (37:25). The meaning is: Or do they—the disbelievers—say, "We are a gathered and united people; we shall take revenge on whoever intends evil for us, or we shall help one another so that we are not defeated"?

His saying, the Exalted: «سَيُهْزَمُ الْجَمْعُ وَيُوَلُّونَ الدُّبُرَ» — Soon shall the confederacy be routed and they shall turn their backs [to flee]. (54:45). The definite article in "the confederacy" (الْجَمْعُ) refers to the previously mentioned group, and in "the backs" (الدُّبُرَ) it denotes the genus. "Turning the back" (تولي الدبر) means retreating. The meaning is: The confederacy they boast of will soon be defeated, and they will turn their backs and flee.

In this verse is a prophecy of the subjugation and defeat of their collective, and an indication that this subjugation would be a defeat in a war they would enter. This occurred in the Battle of Badr, and this is among the prophecies (ملاحم) of the Noble Quran.

His saying, the Exalted: «بَلِ السَّاعَةُ مَوْعِدُهُمْ وَالسَّاعَةُ أَدْهى وَأَمَرُّ» — Indeed, the Hour is their tryst, and the Hour is more calamitous and more bitter. (54:46). "More calamitous" (أَدْهى) is a superlative from al-daha’ (الدهاء), which is the magnitude of a terrible affliction from which there is no way to escape. "More bitter" (أَمَرُّ) is a superlative from bitterness, the opposite of sweetness. In the verse is a transition (إضراب) from threatening them with defeat and worldly punishment to threatening them with what will happen to them at the Hour, the report of which was pointed to at the beginning of the deterrent reports. The speech conveys an intensification (الترقي).

The meaning is: Defeat and worldly punishment are not the full extent of their retribution; rather, the Hour—the report of which We pointed to—is their appointed time, and the Hour is more calamitous than every calamity and more bitter than every bitterness.

His saying, the Exalted: «إِنَّ الْمُجْرِمِينَ فِي ضَلالٍ وَسُعُرٍ» — Indeed the guilty are in error and distress. (54:47). "Distress" (سُعُرٍ) is the plural of sa‘ir (سعير), which is the kindled fire. The verse serves as a justification (تعليل) for what preceded it in His saying: "and the Hour is more calamitous and more bitter." The meaning is: The Hour was only more calamitous and more bitter for them because they are criminals (مجرمون), and criminals are in straying (ضلال) from the abode of felicity—which is Paradise—and in kindled fires.

His saying, the Exalted: «يَوْمَ يُسْحَبُونَ فِي النَّارِ عَلى وُجُوهِهِمْ ذُوقُوا مَسَّ سَقَرَ» — The day they are dragged on their faces into the Fire: ‘Taste the touch of Hell!’ (54:48). "Dragging" (السحب) is to pull a person on their face. "The day" (يَوْمَ) is an adverbial qualifier for His saying: "in error and distress." "Hell" (سَقَرَ) is one of the names of Jahannam, and its "touch" (مسها) is its striking them with its heat and punishment.

The meaning is: Their being in straying and kindled fires occurs on a day they are dragged in the Fire on their faces, and it is said to them: "Taste what Jahannam inflicts upon you of its heat and punishment."

His saying, the Exalted: «إِنَّا كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْناهُ بِقَدَرٍ» — Indeed We have created everything in a measure. (54:49). "Everything" (كُلَّ شَيْءٍ) is in the accusative case due to a suppressed verb indicated by "We created it" (خَلَقْناهُ); the estimation is: "We created everything [that] We created." "In a measure" (بِقَدَرٍ) is connected to His saying "We created it," and the particle ba (بـ) denotes accompaniment. The meaning is: We created everything accompanied by a measure.

The "measure" (قدر) of a thing is the extent (المقدار) which it does not exceed, and the limit and geometry (الحد والهندسة) which it does not overstep in any aspect of increase or decrease. The Exalted said: «وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ» — There is not a thing but its treasuries are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure. (15:21). Thus, for every thing there is a defined limit in its creation that it does not exceed, and an extended path in its existence that it traverses and does not overstep.

The verse is in the position of justifying the punishment of the criminals on the Day of Resurrection mentioned in the two previous verses. It is as if it were asked: Why are the criminals recompensed with straying and kindled fires on the Day of Resurrection and made to taste the touch of Hell? The answer is given by His saying: "Indeed We have created everything in a measure." The gist is that for everything there is a measure (قدر), and part of the measure regarding man is that Allah, the Glorified, created him as a species with numerous individuals through procreation, social in his worldly life, providing himself from his perishing worldly life for his everlasting hereafter. He decreed (قدر) to send a messenger to them to call them to the felicity of this world and the hereafter; whoever responds to the call attains felicity, enters Paradise, and neighbors his Lord, and whoever rejects it and commits crimes is in straying and kindled fires.

It would be an error to say that answering the question in this manner is a form of prohibited circular reasoning (مصادرة) in argumentation. For asking about His, the Exalted, punishing them with Fire for their crimes is in the sense of asking about His decreeing (تقدير) that. Thus, the meaning of the question would be: Why did Allah decree for the criminals the recompense of Fire? And the meaning of the answer would be: Because Allah decreed for the criminals the recompense of Fire. Or the meaning of the question: Why does Allah enter them into the Fire? And the meaning of the answer: Because Allah enters them into the Fire—and that would be a clear circularity.

This is because there is a difference between our action and the action of the Exalted. We, in our actions, follow universal laws and principles (القوانين والأصول الكلية) derived from the external world and concrete existence (الوجود العيني); these govern us in our will and actions. If we eat due to hunger or drink due to thirst, we only intend satiety and quenching because we have acquired from the external world that eating provides satiety and drinking provides quenching—and this is the answer if we were asked about the action.

In short, our actions follow universal rules and general standards (القواعد الكلية والضوابط العامة) abstracted from concrete existence and branching from it. As for the action of the Exalted, it is concrete existence itself. The universal rational principles (الأصول العقلية الكلية) are derived from it, posterior to it, and governed by it; they do not govern it nor precede it. The Exalted said: «لا يُسْئَلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْئَلُونَ» — He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned. (21:23). And He said: «إِنَّ اللهَ يَفْعَلُ ما يَشاءُ» — Indeed Allah does whatever He wishes. (22:18). And He said: «الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ» — The truth is from your Lord. (3:60).

Therefore, there is no questioning His, the Exalted, action with "why" in the sense of asking for an external cause (السبب الخارجي), as there is no cause besides Him to determine His action. Nor is it in the sense of asking for a universal rational principle that validates His action, as rational principles are abstracted from His action and are posterior to it.

Yes, in His speech, the Glorified, the justification of an action occurs in one of three ways:

First: Justifying the action by the goals and benefits (الغايات والفوائد) that result from it, which return to the creation and not to Him. However, this is a justification for the action not insofar as it is His action, the Glorified, but insofar as it is an event occurring in the chain of causes and effects, as in His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَتَجِدَنَّ أَقْرَبَهُمْ مَوَدَّةً لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّا نَصارى ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ مِنْهُمْ قِسِّيسِينَ وَرُهْباناً وَأَنَّهُمْ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ» — ...and you will surely find the nearest of them in affection to the faithful to be those who say, ‘We are Christians.’ That is because there are priests and monks among them, and because they are not arrogant. (5:82). And He said: «وَضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الذِّلَّةُ وَالْمَسْكَنَةُ» — And abasement and destitution were stamped upon them... — until He said — «ذلِكَ بِما عَصَوْا وَكانُوا يَعْتَدُونَ» — ...That was because they would disobey and used to transgress. (2:61).

Second: Justifying His, the Exalted, action by one of His Names and Attributes (أسماءه وصفاته) appropriate to it, such as His justifying the contents of many verses in His speech with phrases like: "Indeed Allah is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful," "and He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise," "and He is the All-Subtle, the All-Aware," and so on. This is common in the Noble Quran. If you reflect deeply on its instances, you will find them to be a justification of an action possessing a specific attribute by a general attribute of His, the Exalted, action. For His active Names (أسماءه الفعلية) are abstracted from His general action. Thus, justifying a specific action by one of His attributes and one of His Names is a justification of the specific aspect of the action by the general aspect within it. For example, His saying, the Exalted: «وَكَأَيِّنْ مِنْ دَابَّةٍ لا تَحْمِلُ رِزْقَهَا اللهُ يَرْزُقُها وَإِيَّاكُمْ وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ» — How many a creature does not carry its own provision! Allah provides for it and for you, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (29:60). He justifies the fulfillment of the needs of creatures and humans for the provision requested by the tongue of their need by the fact that He is All-Hearing, All-Knowing. That is, He created everything while their requests are heard by Him and their states are known to Him, and these are two attributes of His general action. And His saying: «فَتَلَقَّى آدَمُ مِنْ رَبِّهِ كَلِماتٍ فَتابَ عَلَيْهِ إِنَّهُ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ» — Then Adam received certain words from his Lord, and He turned to him clemently. Indeed He is the All-clement, the All-merciful. (2:37). He justifies His turning to Adam by the fact that He is All-Clement, All-Merciful; that is, the attribute of His action is repentance and mercy.

Third: Justifying His specific action by His general action, and its reference is actually to the second way. For example, His saying: «إِنَّ الْمُجْرِمِينَ فِي ضَلالٍ وَسُعُرٍ» — Indeed the guilty are in error and distress. — until He said — «إِنَّا كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْناهُ بِقَدَرٍ» — Indeed We have created everything in a measure. For the "measure" (القدر)—which is a thing being limited such that it does not overstep its limit in the course of its existence—is a general action of His, the Exalted, from which no created thing is exempt. Thus, justifying the punishment by the measure is a justification of His specific action by His general action, and an explanation that it is an instance among the instances of the measure. For it was among what was decreed (المقدر) for man that if he commits a crime by rejecting the call of prophecy, he would be punished and enter the Fire on the Day of Resurrection. Similarly, His saying: «وَإِنْ مِنْكُمْ إِلَّا وارِدُها كانَ عَلى رَبِّكَ حَتْماً مَقْضِيًّا» — There is none of you but will come to it: a decided decree, [already] settled by your Lord. (19:71). He justifies the "coming to it" (الورود) by the "decree" (القضاء), which is a general action of His, while the "coming" is specific in relation to it.

Thus, it becomes clear that what occurs in His speech of justifying one of His actions is only the justification of a specific action by its general attribute, and the cause is a cause for affirmation (للإثبات) [in knowledge] and not for the reality of being (للثبوت) [in the essence of the act], and it is not a form of circular reasoning at all.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَما أَمْرُنا إِلَّا واحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ» — Our command is but a single [word], like the twinkling of an eye. (54:50). It is said in al-Majma‘: "Twinkling" (اللمح) is a quick glance, which is the snatching of the sight. End quote.

What is intended by "command" (الأمر) is that which stands opposite to "prohibition," but it is the existential command (الأمر التكويني) by the will for a thing's existence. The Exalted said: «إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ» — All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. (36:82). Thus, it is the word "Be" (كن), and perhaps because it is a "word" (كلمة), the predicate was treated as feminine, so it was said: "but a single one" (إِلَّا واحِدَةٌ).

What the context provides is that the meaning of the command being "single" is that it does not require multiplicity or repetition for its execution and the realization of its object. Rather, a single command by casting the word "Be" realizes the intended object, like the twinkling of an eye, without delay or hesitation such that it would require the command a second or third time.

The comparison of the command—in terms of the realization of its object—to the twinkling of an eye is not to convey that the time of its effect is short like the time of the twinkling of an eye. Rather, it is to convey that it does not require the passage of time for its effect, even if it were short. For the comparison to the twinkling of an eye in speech is used as a metonymy for that. Thus, His command—which is His bringing into existence and His will for existence—does not require time, space, or motion for its realization. How could it, when time, space, and motion themselves were only realized by His command?

The verse, although by its own purport provides a general truth regarding the creation of things—that their existence, insofar as it is the action of Allah, the Glorified, is like the twinkling of an eye, even if insofar as it is the existence of such-and-such a thing it is gradual, occurring bit by bit—

However, by its occurrence in the context of threatening the disbelievers with the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, it is looking toward the coming of the Hour. It signifies that a single command from Him, the Exalted, is sufficient for the standing of the Hour and the renewal of creation through the resurrection and the gathering. Thus, it completes the argument established by His saying: "Indeed We have created everything in a measure."

The purport of the first verse would then be that their punishment by Fire is in accordance with wisdom and is unavoidable according to the Divine Will because it is part of the measure (القدر). The purport of this verse is that the realization of the Hour in which they are punished—by the execution of this Will and the realization of its object—entails no burden upon Him, the Glorified, because a single command from Him, like the twinkling of an eye, is sufficient for it.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنا أَشْياعَكُمْ فَهَلْ مِنْ مُدَّكِرٍ» — Certainly We have destroyed your likes. So is there anyone who will be admonished? (54:51). "Likes" (الأشياع) is the plural of shi‘ah (شيعة), and what is intended—as has been said—is those similar and comparable in disbelief and denial of the prophets among the past nations.

The purpose of this verse and the two verses after it is to emphasize the previous argument established regarding the inevitable encompassment of the punishment for them.

The summary of the meaning is: What We have warned you of regarding the worldly punishment and the punishment of the Hour is not merely a report We have told you or a word We have cast to you. For these are your likes among the past nations in whom that was enacted; We have destroyed them, which is their punishment in this world, and they shall meet the punishment of the hereafter. For their deeds are written and recorded in books preserved with Us, by which We shall call them to account and recompense them for what they did.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَكُلُّ شَيْءٍ فَعَلُوهُ فِي الزُّبُرِ وَكُلُّ صَغِيرٍ وَكَبِيرٍ مُسْتَطَرٌ» — Everything they have done is in the books, and every small and big [deed] is inscribed. (54:52-53). The "books" (الزبر) are the records of deeds, and interpreting it as the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) is weak. What is intended by "small and big" is the smallness and greatness of deeds, as provided by the context.

His saying, the Exalted: «إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَنَهَرٍ» — Indeed the God-conscious will be amid gardens and rivers, (54:54). That is, in gardens of magnificent status and ultimate description, and a river likewise. It is said: what is intended by "river" (نهر) is the genus, and it is said: "river" is in the sense of vastness (السعة).

His saying, the Exalted: «فِي مَقْعَدِ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَ مَلِيكٍ مُقْتَدِرٍ» — in the abode of truthfulness with an All-powerful King. (54:55). The "abode" (المقعد) is the place of sitting. "King" (المليك) is an intensive form of malik (ملك), as has been said, and it is not from the saturation of the vowel of the lam in malik. The "All-powerful" (المقتدر) is the One of great power, and He is Allah, the Glorified.

What is intended by "truthfulness" (الصدق) is the truthfulness of the God-conscious in their faith and action; the "abode" was annexed to it due to some connection. It is possible that what is intended is that their station and what they possess therein is truth (صدق) unmixed with falsehood. Thus, they have a presence with which there is no absence, a proximity with which there is no distance, a blessing with which there is no misery, a joy with which there is no grief, and a subsistence with which there is no annihilation.

It is also possible that what is intended is the truthfulness of this report, insofar as it is a glad tiding and a beautiful promise for the God-conscious. According to this, there is a kind of contrast between the description of the end of the God-conscious and the criminals. For the criminals were threatened with punishment and straying, and that was established as being from the measure (القدر) and will not fail. And the God-conscious were promised reward and presence with their Lord, the All-Powerful King, and that was established as being truth (صدق) in which there is no lie.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Kamal al-Din, with its chain of narration to ‘Ali ibn Salim from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: I asked him about incantations (الرقى): do they repel anything of the measure (القدر)? He said: "They are from the measure."

And he said: "The Qadariyyah are the Magians of this nation—they are those who wanted to describe Allah by His justice, so they removed Him from His sovereignty. Regarding them, this verse was revealed: «يَوْمَ يُسْحَبُونَ فِي النَّارِ عَلى وُجُوهِهِمْ ـ ذُوقُوا مَسَّ سَقَرَ إِنَّا كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْناهُ بِقَدَرٍ» — The day they are dragged on their faces into the Fire: ‘Taste the touch of Hell! Indeed We have created everything in a measure.’"

I say: What is intended by the "Qadariyyah" are those who deny the measure (القدر), and they are the Mu‘tazilah who believe in delegation (التفويض). His saying: "they are the Magians of this nation" is because of their saying that the creator of voluntary actions is man, and Allah is the creator of what is beyond that; thus they affirmed two gods, just as the Magians affirmed two gods: a creator of good and a creator of evil.

His saying: "they wanted to describe Allah by His justice, so they removed Him from His sovereignty" is because they spoke of man's creation of his own actions to flee from the doctrine of compulsion (الجبر) which contradicts justice. Thus, they removed Allah from His sovereignty over the actions of His servants by severing their attribution to Him, the Exalted.

His saying: "Regarding them, this verse was revealed," etc., what is intended is the application (جري) of the verses to them, rather than their being the specific occasion of revelation (سبب النزول) or its primary instance. This is because you have known in the commentary of the verses that they are general according to the context. Regarding the revelation of the verses about them, there are other narrations reported from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them), and from the paths of the Sunnis as well, there are narrations to this effect from Ibn ‘Abbas, Ibn ‘Umar, Muhammad ibn Ka‘b, and others.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad reported from Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Indeed, for every nation there are Magians—and the Magians of this nation are those who say: 'There is no measure (قدر).'" The report.

I say: It was also narrated in Thawab al-A‘mal, with its chain to al-Sadiq from his fathers from ‘Ali (peace be upon him), and its wording is: "For every nation there are Magians—and the Magians of this nation are those who say: 'There is no measure (قدر).'"

In it [the same source], Ibn Mardawayh reported with a chain narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "The river (النهر) is space and vastness; it is not a flowing river."

In it, Abu Nu‘aym reported from Jabir, who said: While the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was one day in the mosque of Madinah, some of his companions mentioned Paradise. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "O Abu Dujanah, do you not know that whoever loves us and is tested with our love, Allah the Exalted will settle him with us?" Then he recited: «فِي مَقْعَدِ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَ مَلِيكٍ مُقْتَدِرٍ» — in the abode of truthfulness with an All-powerful King.

In Ruh al-Ma‘ani, regarding His saying: «فِي مَقْعَدِ صِدْقٍ» — in the abode of truthfulness, Ja‘far al-Sadiq (may Allah be pleased with him) said: "The place was praised with truthfulness (الصدق), so none shall sit in it except the people of truthfulness."

(A Discourse on the Measure)

The "measure" (القدر)—which is the geometry of a thing and the limit of its existence—is something whose mention is repeated in His, the Exalted, speech where He speaks regarding the matter of creation. The Exalted said: «وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ» — There is not a thing but its treasuries are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure. (15:21). Its apparent meaning is that the measure is inseparable from the "sending down" (الإنزال) from the treasuries existing with Him, the Exalted. As for the treasuries themselves—which are undoubtedly from His origination (إبداعه)—they are not measured by this measure that accompanies the sending down. "Sending down" is His issuing it to this witnessed world, as provided by His saying: «وَأَنْزَلْنَا الْحَدِيدَ» — And We sent down iron (57:25), and His saying: «وَأَنْزَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَنْعامِ ثَمانِيَةَ أَزْواجٍ» — and He sent down for you eight pairs of livestock (39:6).

This is supported by what has been reported of the interpretation of the measure as being like breadth, length, and other physical, natural limits and characteristics. As in al-Mahasin, from his father from Yunus from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him), who said: "Nothing occurs except what Allah wills (شاء), intends (أراد), measures (قدر), and decrees (قضى)." I said: "What is the meaning of 'willed'?" He said: "He began the action." I said: "What is the meaning of 'intended'?" He said: "The establishment upon it." I said: "What is the meaning of 'measured'?" He said: "The measuring of the thing in its length and breadth." I said: "What is the meaning of 'decreed'?" He said: "When He decrees, He executes it; that is what cannot be repelled."

This meaning was also narrated from his father from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr from Muhammad ibn Ishaq from al-Rida (peace be upon him) in a detailed report, in which he said: "Do you know what 'measure' (قدر) is?" He said: "No." He said: "It is the geometry (الهندسة) of length, breadth, and duration." The report.

From here it becomes clear that what is intended by "everything" in His saying: «وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيراً» — He created everything and ordained it in a [precise] measure (25:3), and His saying: «إِنَّا كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْناهُ بِقَدَرٍ» — Indeed We have created everything in a measure (54:49), and His saying: «وَكُلُّ شَيْءٍ عِنْدَهُ بِمِقْدارٍ» — and everything is with Him by a measure (13:8), and His saying: «الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى» — He who gave everything its creation and then guided it (20:50), refers to the things occurring in our witnessed world—the natural things subject to creation and composition. Or, that the "measuring" (التقدير) has two levels:

A level that encompasses everything other than Allah, which is the limitation of the origin of existence by possibility (الإمكان) and need (الحاجة); this encompasses all existents except Allah, the Glorified. The Exalted said: «وَكانَ اللهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ مُحِيطاً» — and Allah encompasses all things. (4:126).

And a level specific to our witnessed world, which is the limitation of the existence of things found within it—in terms of their existence, the effects of their existence, and the characteristics of their being—insofar as their existence and effects are dependent on matters external to them of causes and conditions. Thus, their existence and states vary with the variation of their causes and conditions. They are molded by molds from within and without that determine for them the breadth, length, shape, form, and other states and actions that suit them.

Thus, the measuring (التقدير) guides this type of existents to what was measured for them in the course of their existence. The Exalted said: «الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى» — who created and proportioned, who determined and guided (87:2-3); that is, He guided what He created to what He measured for it. Then, He completed that by the execution of the decree (القضاء). In this sense is His saying regarding man: «مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ خَلَقَهُ فَقَدَّرَهُ ثُمَّ السَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُ» — From a drop of [seminal] fluid He created him and then proportioned him. Then He made the way easy for him. (80:19-20). He points by His saying: "Then He made the way easy for him" to the fact that the measuring (التقدير) does not contradict the voluntariness of his voluntary actions.

This type of measure (القدر) is in itself different from the decree (القضاء), which is the final judgment (الحكم البتي) from Him, the Exalted, for its existence: «وَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ لا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ» — Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment. (13:41). For sometimes something is measured but the decree does not follow it, such as the measure necessitated by some external causes and conditions which then becomes void due to an obstacle or by the substitution of another cause. The Exalted said: «يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ» — Allah effaces and confirms whatever He wishes (13:39), and He said: «ما نَنْسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنْسِها نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِنْها أَوْ مِثْلِها» — For any verse We abrogate or cause to be forgotten, We bring another which is better than it, or its like. (2:106). And sometimes it is measured and the decree follows it, as when it is measured from all sides by the gathering of all its causes and conditions and the removal of its obstacles.

To this points his (peace be upon him) saying in the previous report of al-Mahasin: "When He decrees, He executes it; that is what cannot be repelled." Close to this is what is in a number of reports of the decree and the measure (القضاء والقدر), the meaning of which is that the measure can fail to occur, but as for the decree, it is not repelled.

And from ‘Ali (peace be upon him) through various paths, as in al-Tawhid, with its chain to Ibn Nubatah: that the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) moved away from a leaning wall to another wall. It was said to him: "O Commander of the Faithful, do you flee from the decree (قضاء) of Allah?" He said: "I flee from the decree (قضاء) of Allah to the measure (قدر) of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

As for the first type of existents, whose measure is merely the limit of its existence in terms of its possibility and need, the measure (القدر) and the decree (القضاء) in it are one, and the measure in it never fails to be realized.

Rational inquiry supports what has preceded. For matters that have composite causes consisting of an agent, matter, conditions, preparatives, and obstacles—each of these has an effect on the thing according to what is homogeneous with it. It is like the mold by which the thing is molded, so it takes for itself the form of its mold and its characteristic; this is its measure (قدر). Then, when the complete cause (العللة التامة) gathers its parts, it grants it the necessity of existence (ضرورة الوجود); this is the decree (القضاء) that cannot be repelled. A discussion on the decree has already preceded in the commentary of the beginning of Surah al-Isra’, which is not without benefit in this inquiry; so let it be referred to.
